coredata.dc:title: Influence of lithiation on electro-optical properties of disk-like coronene molecule
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Forozmand, Niko
Biglari, Zeinab
coredata.dc:description: The electronic properties, the first hyperpolarizability (β 0) and the second hyperpolarizability (γ0) of coronene nanodisk (C24H12) and its doped structures with lithium atoms have been investigated employing the density functional theory (DFT). The influence of the number and position of lithium atoms has been investigated and 12 stable structures are obtained from this process. The energy gap values (E g) of lithiated structures reduced (32–92%) compared to the pristine coronene. The lithiation process improves the nonlinear optical (NLO) properties of structures and causes the remarkable enhancement of the β 0 and γ0 values of them. The β 0 value of Li4C24H4-1235 structure is 28962.1 au and the γ0 value of Li6C24 is 4380×103 au. The time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations are also carried out to understand how lithiation affects the NLO properties. The results of this study may be valuable for further theoretical and experimental studies on the electronic and nonlinear optical properties of 2-Dimentional nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coronene
Lithiation
Hyperpolarizability
DFT calculations
NLO material

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Linear and nonlinear optical properties of structures

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Lorestan University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The optimized structures and vibrational spectra of coronene.
The total density of state (TDOS) diagram with Eg value in eV and UV–Visible spectra of coronene.
The optimized, vibrational spectra and TDOS diagrams with Eg value (eV) of lithiated structures.
The HOMO–LUMO pictures of considered structures with spin multiplicity 1, The SOMO-α-occupied, SOMO-β-unoccupied and LUMO pictures of the considered structures with spin multiplicity 2.
Crucial spectral line display of UV–visible spectra of all lithiated structures of coronene.
UV–visible spectra of lithiated structures of coronene with crucial spectral line greater than 800 nm.
The obtained bond lengths and the most important vibrational frequencies of coronene with their intensities.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The experimental values; the values in parentheses are from Ref. [53].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The calculated dipole moment (μ), polarizability (α), the first hyperpolarizability (β 0) and the second hyperpolarizability (γ0) of considered structures.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: These values are from Ref. [22] and Ref. [55], respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The β 0 values of structures with more than 3000 au and some components from previous reports.

The calculated transition energy (ΔE), the difference of dipole moment (Δμ) between the ground state and the crucial excited state, the largest oscillator strength (f 0) and the main compositions of the crucial transition state of the considered structures.

row.entry: electron transitions of crucial excited states

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The largest oscillator strengths (f 0), absorption wavelengths (λ) and electron transitions of the other excited states of studied structures.

head.ce:title: Influence of lithiation on electro-optical properties of disk-like coronene molecule

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Lorestan University, Khorramabad, Lorestan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Lorestan University, Khorramabad, Lorestan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electronic properties, the first hyperpolarizability (β 0) and the second hyperpolarizability (γ0) of coronene nanodisk (C24H12) and its doped structures with lithium atoms have been investigated employing the density functional theory (DFT). The influence of the number and position of lithium atoms has been investigated and 12 stable structures are obtained from this process. The energy gap values (E g) of lithiated structures reduced (32–92%) compared to the pristine coronene. The lithiation process improves the nonlinear optical (NLO) properties of structures and causes the remarkable enhancement of the β 0 and γ0 values of them. The β 0 value of Li4C24H4-1235 structure is 28962.1 au and the γ0 value of Li6C24 is 4380×103 au. The time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations are also carried out to understand how lithiation affects the NLO properties. The results of this study may be valuable for further theoretical and experimental studies on the electronic and nonlinear optical properties of 2-Dimentional nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a sheet of carbon atoms as thick as a one-atom arranged in a honeycomb pattern that are bound together by overlapping sp2 hybrid bonds. The Nobel Prize in Physics 2010 was awarded jointly to Andre Geim and Kostya Novoselov “for groundbreaking experiments regarding the two-dimensional material graphene” [1,2]. After several years of extensive research, graphene remains an important subject of theoretical and experimental research and it is important for practical applications in nano-electronics and material science [3–7]. Graphene quantum dots (GQD), which belong to the graphene family, are segments of graphene with various shapes and sizes at the nanoscale with closed edges (with hydrogen). They have been extensively studied by a great number of theoretical calculations and synthesized in laboratory. GQDs are used in a variety of technological fields due to their various geometric structures and unique electronic properties. Doping and functionalizing of GQDs can create properties suitable for specific applications in them [8–12].

The second generation harmonic (SHG) was discovered in 1961 by Franken et al., and since then the study of nonlinear optical (NLO) materials attracted the attention of scientific research [13]. Over the past several decades, we have witnessed great developments in the field of NLO materials and their new applications. Efficient optical materials have several potential applications in electronic devices, semiconductors, organic light emitting diodes (OLEDs) and solar cells [14–20]. On the other hand, the NLO materials have many applications in laser frequency modulation, laser protective devices, holographic imaging, dynamic image processing, modern optical data storage, optical computing, frequency mixing, telecommunications, sensing, microfabrication, cancer therapy and so forth [21–35]. Over the decades, computational chemistry methods have greatly changed the design and development knowledge of NLO molecular materials so that experimental researchers can use the insights that such computations provide to effectively guide synthetic strategies. The design and synthesis of new materials with large second-order nonlinear optical properties has attracted much attention in the experimental and theoretical fields. In particular, organic nonlinear optical materials that have unique properties such as large optical sensitivities [36–41]. Various strategies are used to enhance the NLO response of materials. One of the effective approaches to enhance the NLO properties is excess electron. It was reported that excess electrons can significantly enhance the NLO properties of organic and inorganic systems [42–44]. In some compounds, alkali atoms (especially lithium, which is called the lithiation process) play as dopant and excess electrons are transferred to the system through chemical doping to form organometallic compounds. In alkali metal-doped organic materials, alkali atoms are bonded to carbon skeletons by covalent bonging. There are reports of an increase the first static hyperpolarizability (β 0) of some systems due to the lithiation process [45–51].

Coronene (C24H12), a GQD, is a polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) composed of seven concentric benzene rings with a D6h symmetry and is known as superbenzene (Fig. 1 ). The crystal and molecular structure of coronene has been identified and determined by some groups of researchers [52–56]. Recently, this molecule has been the subject of much research and its electronic and optical properties have been studied [57–65].

In this work, the influence of the lithiation process on the electronic and NLO properties of the coronene nanodisk is investigated through DFT and TD-DFT calculations. The influence of the number of lithium atoms and their locations on electro-optical properties of coronene has also been investigated. The results could provide new insights into the possibility of novel NLO materials. We hope that such a theoretical study could provide useful information about NLO materials to experimentalists.

All structures considered in this study were fully optimized at B3LYP method [66,67] using 6-311+G(d,p) basis set [68–70] in Gaussian 09 program [71]. The optimized geometries were followed by calculations and since no imaginary frequency is observed, it is verified that the optimized structures are minimal on the potential energy surfaces. The HOMO-LUMO energy gap (E g) was calculated in terms of the obtained energies of the highest occupied molecular orbital (ε
H ) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (ε
L ) as:
(1)
E g
=
ε L
−
ε H

It should be noted that in the calculation of E
g for the systems in a doublet state, the singly occupied molecular orbital (SOMO) is considered. The interaction of the electromagnetic field with molecules in the material will create a polarization. The macroscopic polarization (P) of the material can be expressed as the following equation:
(2)
P =
χ
( 1 )
E +
χ
( 2 )
E . E +
χ
( 3 )
E . E . E + …
where E is the applied electronic field intensity, the coefficient χ (1), χ (2) and χ (3) are the first, second and third-order polarizability of the macroscopic material. When a weak electronic field interacts with a material, the electric polarization of the material changes linearly with the intensity of the initial electric field, so we can write:
(3)
P =
χ
( 1 )
E

But many of the recently discovered laser phenomena cannot be explained by the principles of linear optics. Therefore, in the interaction of a strong electronic field with material, the nonlinear coefficients, χ (2) and χ (3) are large enough and must be considered. The molecular electric polarization (P′) for microscopic molecules with the electric field intensity E has the following relationship [20,22]:
(4)
P ′
=
α
i j
E j
+
β
i j k
E j
.
E k
+
γ
i j k l
E j
.
E k
.
E l
+ …

In this equation, α
ij is the ij component of the polarizability tensor (α), β
ijk is the ijk component of the first hyperpolarizability tensor (β) and γ ijkl is the ijkl component of the second hyperpolarizability tensor (γ). Calculating the nonlinear microscopic polarization coefficient of β or γ of the molecules is a very important measurement for the design of a high-performance NLO material in quantum chemistry studies. When a molecule is exposed to the static electric field (F), the energy (E) of the molecule is expressed by the following equation:
(5)
E =
E
( 0 )
−
μ i
F i
−
1
2 !
α
i j
F i
F j
−
1
3 !
β
i j k
F i
F j
F k
−
1
4 !
γ
i j k l
F i
F j
F k
F l
− …
where E (0) is the energy of the molecule in the absence of an electronic field, μ
i is the component of the dipole moment vector, α
ij is the linear polarizability tensor, β and γ are the first and second hyperpolarizability tensors whereas i, j, k and l label with x, y and z components, respectively. The average dipole moment μ 0, polarizability α 0, first hyperpolarizability (β 0) and second hyperpolarizability (γ0) are defined as follows:
(6)
μ 0
=
(
μ x 2
+
μ y 2
+
μ z 2
)
1 2
(7)
α 0
=
1 3
(
α
x x
+
α
y y
+
α
z z
)
(8)
β 0
=
(
β x 2
+
β y 2
+
β z 2
)
1 2
(9)
γ 0
=
1 5
(
(
γ
x x x x
+
γ
y y y y
+
γ
z z z z
)
+ 2
(
γ
x x y y
+
γ
x x z z
+
γ
y y z z
)
)

The first hyperpolarizability (β 0) and the second hyperpolarizability (γ0), known as nonlinear optical response (NLO) coefficients, are calculated by an analytical density functional Coulomb-attenuated hybrid exchange-correlation functional CAM-B3LYP [72] approach and 6-311+G (d,p) basis set.

All of considered structures were fully optimized using the B3LYP method and 6-311+G(d,p) basis set. These structural stability is confirmed using the real values in vibrational calculations at the same computational level. The optimized geometry and vibrational spectra of coronene (C24H12) are displayed in Fig. 1. Coronene has a centrosymmetric planar structure with D6h symmetry, which has a central six-membered aromatic ring and six benzene rings around it.

Coronene is part of graphene and has a hexagonal zigzag nanodisk structure. Based on the optimized structure, bond lengths and the most important vibrational frequency are presented in Table 1 . The lowest vibrational frequency is 89.8 cm−1 and it indicate which is at the minimum potential energy surface. Infrared (IR) vibrational spectrum can represent geometric structure information. There are four peaks with relatively higher intensities at 576, 876, 1337 and 3177 cm−1 in IR spectrum of coronene, are related to the vibrational modes. In studying the IR spectrum, we find that the vibrational mode at 875 is related to the bending vibration of the C–H bonds. In addition, the peak at 3177 cm−1 is the asymmetric stretching vibration mode of the C–H band.

These two peaks have completely disappeared in the Li6C24 structure. The obtained bond lengths in this work and the first three peaks are in good agreement with the results previously reported by Sun et al. [59] and experimental values [53]. The length of central bonds 1.418 Å and the mean length of C–C bonds 1.406 Å reported by Coulson [52]. TD-DFT calculations were performed to obtain the UV–Visible spectra of considered molecules. The total density of state (TDOS) diagram and UV–Visible adsorption spectra of coronene are displayed in Fig. 2 . Energy gap (E
g = 4.0 eV) and the location of HOMO (−5.78 eV) and LUMO (−1.78 eV) energies are shown in DOS diagram of coronene. These values are also in good agreement with the values reported in Ref. [59]. In UV–visible spectra of coronene, a relatively strong peak is observed at 271.0 nm, which is in UV region and related to electron transition to dominant excited state.

The six benzene rings of coronene at their rim positions of C–C bonds can be lithiated thus each of these bands is lithiated separately. Structures with two, three and four Li atoms have three isomers which the difference among them is the position of Li atoms. Thus, 12 stable doped structures are obtained. The optimized structures, vibrational spectra and TDOS diagrams are shown in Fig. 3 . In the vibrational spectrum of doped structures, the peak intensity decreases at ~875 and 3180 cm−1 with reducing the number of H atoms and reach to zero in the Li6C24 structure. These peaks, as mentioned, are related to bending and asymmetric stretching vibrational modes of the C–H bonds, respectively. The vibrational frequency calculations were also performed to confirm that these geometries were within the minimum potential energy surface. Since no imaginary frequencies are observed in their vibrational spectrum, it can be concluded that these structures are stable. These structures are considered for studying the electro-optical properties.

The multiplicity, HOMO and LUMO energies (ε H and ε L ), energy gap (Eg), percentage of variation of Eg (%ΔE g) and the lowest vibrational frequency (νlow.) of optimized structures at B3LYP/6-311+G(d,p) level of theory are listed in Table 2 . The E g value of pristine coronene is 4.0 eV and for lithiated coronenes is among 0.32–2.71 eV. It is show that the effect of the lithiation process on the E g of the coronene is significant. The E g of all structures are reduced and are narrowed about 92–32%. The lowest value of E g (0.32 eV) is related to Li4C24H4-1235 structure with 92% decrease. The total density of state (TDOS) plot of all doped structures is displayed in Fig. 3. The decrease of E g values and narrowing of HOMO-LUMO gap is observed in all DOS plots of lithiated structures.

The decrease of E g value is due to the formation of new HOMO and LUMO levels compared to the original levels of pristine coronene. Electrical conductivity can be expressed in terms of energy gap with the following expression [73]:
(10)
σ
∝ exp
( −
E g
/ k T )
where σ represents the electrical conductivity, k is the Boltzmann's constant, and T represents the absolute temperature. Therefore, at a constant temperature, the electrical conductivity will increase as the energy gap (E g) decreases. The reduction in energy gap is observed in all doped structures, so the conductivity of all structures increases relative to the origin molecule, but this reduction is stronger in structures with even numbers of lithium atoms. Reducing the energy gap brings the valance level and the conduction level closer and increases the probability of the presence of electrons at the Fermi level. Therefore, increasing the conductivity and decreasing the energy gap in these structures causes the appearance and observation of density peaks in the Fermi region in total density of state (TDOS) diagrams.

The picture of HOMO and LUMO distributions for singlet state; and SOMO-α-occupied, SOMO-β-unoccupied and LUMO for doublet states of the studied systems are presented in Fig. 4 . According to Fig. 4, it is clear that Li atoms are involved in HOMO-LUMO distributions; therefore the effect of lithiation process on the narrowing HLG of the studied compounds is confirmed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear and nonlinear optical properties of structures
ce:section.ce:para: The average dipole moment (μ 0), polarizability (α 0), the first static hyperpolarizability (β 0) and the second hyperpolarizability (γ0) values of considered structures are calculated at CAM-B3LYP/6–311++G (d,p) level of theory and are listed in Table 3 . The μ 0, α 0 , β 0 and γ0 values of coronene are 0.0 D, 291.1 au, 0.0 au and 87×103 au, respectively. This molecule has a planar structure with D6h symmetry and also symmetric electron distribution on its two surfaces. The dipole moment values of Li2C24H8-14, Li3C24H6-135 and Li6C24 structures are also 0.0 D. These structures have D2h, D3h and D6h symmetry, respectively.

The zero dipole moment of these molecules is due to their high symmetry. The large value of μ is significant for some systems, such as Li4C24H4-1235 = 12.8 > Li3C24H6-123 = 10.7 > Li4C24H4-1234 = 10.4 > Li2C24H8-12 = 9.3 D. These values and the study of their related structures show that charge transfer (CT) and electron diffusion take place from the lithium atoms to the carbon nanodisk. The amount of charge transfer (CT) from each lithium atom to the carbon nanodisk is approximately 0.885.

The polarizability value of all lithiated coronene are ranging from 321.7 for LiC24H10 to 760.3 au for Li4C24H4-1235 and all of them are larger than coronene ones. The largest value of polarizability (α = 760.3 au) is related to Li4C24H4-1235 structure. The β 0 values of Li2C24H8-14, Li3C24H6-135 and Li6C24 structures are 77.2, 5.3 and 45.7 au, which is a very small increase in the β 0 value, due to the high symmetry of the systems. While the calculated values of the third-order NLO response, γ0, of these structures are 1.43×106, 3.88×105 and 4.38×106 au, respectively. Based on results in Table 3, several lithiated structures have a significant increase in β 0 value. The Li4C24H4-1235, Li2C24H8-13, Li4C24H4-1234, Li2C24H8-12, Li3C24H6-123, Li5C24H2, Li3C24H6-124, LiC24H10 and Li4C24H4-1245 structures have β 0 value 28962.1, 9717.0, 8977.1, 8122.8, 6353.7, 3843.1, 3171.2, 2661.5 and 1539.9 au. The highest value of β 0 (28962.1 au) is related to Li4C24H4-1235 structure. The results indicate that lithiation process lead to the increment of the first static hyperpolarizability of coronene nanodisk. Also, when lithium atoms are symmetrically attached to the coronene and form a structure with high symmetry, they have the least influence on increasing of β 0 value and asymmetric lithiated structures have a significant increase in β 0 value. The β 0 values of structures with more than 3000 au and some components from previous reports are listed for comparison in Table 4 . The results show the β 0 values of Li4C24H4-1235 is 28,962.1 au which is larger than the 10645 and 6376 au of LiCN/Li@BNNT with two different orientation of LiCN/Li in BNNT [37], and also larger than 7326 au of the reported cup-like electride Li@calix [4] pyrrole [41]. Also, the β 0 value of Li4C24H4-1235 is greater than that of the lithiated compounds including: Lin-[n]-cyclacene (n = 5, 6, 7, 8) = 7938, 7049, 8317, 15497 au [45,46]; Li-Adamantane = 11293 au [49]; Li3-sumanene = 2919, Li3-corannulene = 4940, Li4-Quadronulene = 4451 au [48,51]; Li5-BN-CNT-2a = 14200 au [50] and three N-doped nanographene (2660, 2050, 28300 au) [55]. Three Li5-BN-CNT heterojunction nanotubes have the β 0 values 32200, 60300, 69700 au [50] which are greater than the β 0 value of Li4C24H4-1235.

The calculated values of the third-order nonlinear polarizability for coronene and its Lin-doped derivatives are presented in Table 3. Results show that the Lin-doped coronene compounds have large amplitudes of third-order nonlinear polarizability ranging from 44×10−36 to 2208×10−36 esu (87×103 ‒ 4380 ×103 au). However, theoretical studies have shown that C60 compounds have amplitudes of third-order nonlinear polarizability ranging from 4.94 to 7.00 ×10−36 esu [20]. As well as, the types of graphene nanoflakes with different shapes have the γ0 values ranging from 21×103 ‒ 3410×103 au [22,55]. The studied structures in this work are potential candidates for nonlinear optical properties due to the transfer of electrons from the alkali metal to the coronene skeleton.

To get a better view of how lithiation process affects the first hyperpolarizability of coronene nanodisk, the time dependent density functional method (TD-DFT) calculations were carried out to identify the crucial excited state, which has the greatest oscillator strength among the considered excited states, and the conventional two-level model was used as follow [74–77]:
(11)
β 0
∝
Δ μ
f 0
Δ
Ε 3

In this equation, f 0, ΔE and Δμ are oscillator strength, the transition energy and difference in the dipole moments between the ground state and the crucial excited state. These calculations were performed with TD-DFT method at CAM-B3LYP/6-311+G(d) level of theory. The obtained f 0, Δμ, ΔE, absorption wavelength (λ) and electron transitions major compositions of the crucial excited state for all structures are listed in Table 5 . Investigation of the TD-DFT calculations and the results of Table 5 show that the coronene molecule has an electron excitation energy (ΔE) with 4.575 eV, Δμ (9.072 D) and f 0 (1.017), which the wavelength of it appear at 271.0 nm in the UV region as shown in Fig. 2. The ΔE of the doped structures is among 0.636 ─ 2.418 eV, so it is clear that the ΔE of the coronene is greater than those of all doped structures. Therefore, we expect that the UV–Visible absorption peaks in lithiated structures shift to larger wavelengths in the visible or IR region. The UV–Visible spectrum of doped structures is shown in Fig. 5 . As the results show, structures with odd number of Li atoms have only one significant electron transition with the highest oscillator strength in the UV–Visible region.

Structures with even numbers of Li atoms have amplitudes in Δμ (1.367–9.057 D), f 0 (0.022–0.165 au), and ΔE (0.636–1.871 eV), while for structures with an odd number of lithium atoms, these amplitudes are 0.304–1.116 D, 0.018–0.061 au and 2.219–2.418 eV, respectively.
The results of the calculations show that structures with even numbers of lithium atoms have larger NLO responses and larger β 0 values due to their larger Δμ and f 0 as well as smaller ΔE.

The result of UV–Visible adsorption data shows that the λmax of crucial excited state of all doped-structures are shifted to a minimum of ~513 to a maximum of ~4200 nm. Structures with even number of Li atoms have λmax > 800 nm, that is, they appear in the IR region of the electromagnetic radiation spectrum. In order to further investigate of the UV–Visible absorption spectrum of compounds, we decided to identify and report the important peaks of the spectra with large oscillation strength. The results are listed in Table 6 and their absorption spectra are shown in Fig. 6 . Structures with even number of Li atoms have crucial excited state transition with the highest oscillator strength in IR region and also they have a peak with relatively high oscillator strength in the UV–Visible region.

However, the lithiation process has a significant effect on the first hyperpolarizability of coronene, and as expected, the excess electron of Li metal is transferred to the coronene nanodisk, which can affect the electronic and NLO properties of the designed structures.

The current DFT study shows that the electro-optical properties of coronene are significantly sensitive to the interaction with Li atoms. The number of Li atoms and location of them are effective on electro-optical properties and β 0 and γ0 values of coronene. Three stable structures are obtained with two, three and four Li atoms, respectively. The lithiation process narrows the E g of coronene nanodisk and increases its conductivity properties. As a result of the charge transfer (CT) from the Li atoms to the nanodisk, the dipole moment of some doped structures (1.6–12.8 D) are larger than pristine coronene (0.0 D). Some of obtained structures have with high symmetry and zero dipole moment. Lithiated structures Li4C24H2-1235 have the highest second-order NLO response (β 0 = 28962.1 au) and Li6C24 have the highest third-order NLO response (γ0 = 4380×103 au). Therefore, it can be concluded that lithiated coronene could be a potential suitable suggestion for new NLO material.
Niko Forozmand: M. Sc. student, computations, preparation of figures and tables, Zeinab Biglari: Supervisor, Writing – original draft preparation, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Lorestan University.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electric field effect in atomically thin carbon films

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.S. Novoselov, A.K. Geim, S.V. Morozov, D. Jiang, Y. Zhang, S.V. Dubonos, I.V. Grigorieva, A.A. Firsob, Electric Field Effect in Atomically Thin Carbon Films. Science 306 (2004) 666-669. DOI:10.1126/science.1102896.
A.K. Geim, K.S. Novoselov, The Rise of Graphene. Nat. Mater. 6 (2007) 183-191. https://doi.org/10.1038/nmat1849.
Z. Sun, D.K. James, J.M. Tour, Graphene Chemistry: Synthesis and Manipulation, J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 2 (2011) 2425-2432. https://doi.org/10.1021/jz201000a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electronic functionality in graphene-based nanoarchitectures: discovery and design via first-principles modeling

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Scalable production of nanographene and doping via nondestructive covalent functionalization

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functionalized nanographene sheets with high antiviral activity through synergistic electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I.S. Donskyi, W. Azab, M.F. Gholami, G. Algara-Siller, F. Witte, A. Lippitz, W.E.S. Unger, B. Paulus, J.P. Rabe, M. Adeli, R. Haag, Functionalized nanographene sheets with high antiviral activity through synergistic electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions, Nanoscale, 11 (2019) 15804-15809. https://doi.org/10.1039/C9NR05273A.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene and its derivatives for laser protection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Chen, T. Bai, N. Dong, F. Fan, S. Zhang, X. Zhuang, J. Sun, B. Zhang, X. Zhang, J. Wang, W.J. Blau, Graphene and its derivatives for laser protection. Prog. Mater. Sci. 84 (2016) 118-157. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pmatsci.2016.09.003
W.L. Wang, S. Meng, E. Kaxiras, Graphene nanoflakes with large spin, Nano Lett. 8 (2008) 241-245. https://doi.org/10.1021/nl072548a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metallic graphene nanodisks: electronic and magnetic properties

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Shape effect of graphene quantum dots on enhancing second-order nonlinear optical response and spin multiplicity in NH2–GQD–NO2 systems
